eventTitle: First orbital flight and runway return of a Shuttle orbiter

evidence.locator: Mission Facts, Mission Objectives, and Mission Highlights sections
evidence.factSummary: NASA flew Columbia to orbit on April 12 and returned the orbiter and crew to a runway landing on April 14.

discoveryNote: The orbiter returned, but the external tank was expendable; later mapping must treat this as partial-system reuse.

eventTitle: Columbia completed a second orbital mission

evidence.locator: Mission overview, Mission Facts, and Mission Objectives sections
evidence.factSummary: NASA launched Columbia on its second mission and landed it two days later, demonstrating orbiter relaunch and return.

discoveryNote: This demonstrates reuse of the orbiter, not reuse of every launch-vehicle stage.

eventTitle: Challenger and its crew were lost during ascent

evidence.locator: Notable Missions section, The Flight of STS-51L entry
evidence.factSummary: Space Shuttle Challenger was lost 73 seconds after launch, and all seven crew members died.

discoveryNote: Negative evidence for reusable crew transport; later review should distinguish safety setback from technology dormancy.

eventTitle: Discovery restored Shuttle flight operations after the Challenger hiatus

evidence.locator: Opening mission account and system-modification discussion
evidence.factSummary: Discovery launched and landed on STS-26, returning the Shuttle program to flight after a 32-month hiatus and extensive system changes.

discoveryNote: Recovery milestone following a fatal setback; it does not erase the preceding interruption.

eventTitle: Columbia and its crew were lost during reentry

evidence.locator: Notable Missions section, Columbia STS-107 entry
evidence.factSummary: Columbia suffered a catastrophic failure during reentry, and the orbiter and all seven crew members were lost.

discoveryNote: Negative evidence for reusable crew transport and reentry safety; later mapping must not convert the retrospective page date into the event date.

eventTitle: An orbital-class first stage returned to a landing pad

evidence.locator: Explanation paragraph describing the prior week's launch and landing
evidence.factSummary: A Falcon 9 first stage returned to a Florida landing pad while the upper stage continued placing satellites into orbit.

discoveryNote: Important partial-reuse milestone; it is not evidence of full-vehicle reuse or of a stage reflight.

eventTitle: Falcon Heavy reached orbit with partial booster recovery

evidence.locator: Opening mission account and booster-landing discussion
evidence.factSummary: Falcon Heavy completed its demonstration launch and landed both side boosters, while the center core was not recovered.

discoveryNote: One occurrence contains both a successful launch and partial recovery failure; keep it as one candidate rather than two duplicates.

eventTitle: Falcon first-stage recovery reached a three-digit operational count

evidence.locator: Commercial Resupply post body
evidence.factSummary: A Falcon 9 first stage landed on a droneship after launch, which NASA recorded as SpaceX's 100th successful first-stage landing and recovery.

discoveryNote: Evidence of repeated first-stage recovery; the count does not establish full-vehicle reuse.

eventTitle: A commercial crew launch returned its booster to a landing zone

evidence.locator: Commercial Crew Program post body
evidence.factSummary: The Falcon 9 first stage used for Crew-7 returned to Landing Zone 1, the first commercial crew flight booster to return there for recovery.

discoveryNote: Connects first-stage recovery with a crew mission, but does not itself prove crew escape capability or full-stack reuse.

eventTitle: The first integrated Starship launch ended in vehicle loss

evidence.locator: Page 1, opening incident and mishap-investigation paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: During the first integrated Starship-Super Heavy launch, the vehicle left its expected trajectory and was destroyed after the flight safety system activated.

discoveryNote: FAA classified the launch as a mishap; this candidate records the flight outcome rather than the later investigation closure.

eventTitle: Robotic Refueling Mission installed at its permanent ISS site

evidence.locator: Launch and Mounting on ISS section
evidence.factSummary: Canadarm2 and Dextre moved the RRM module to its permanent ISS location on ExPRESS Logistics Carrier-4.

discoveryNote: Installation enabled later demonstrations but is not itself evidence that orbital refueling was completed.

eventTitle: RRM demonstrated robotic fluid transfer in orbit

evidence.locator: Release 13-046 opening and six-day activity paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Ground controllers used the ISS Dextre robot during six days in January 2013 to complete a first-of-its-kind orbital fluid-transfer demonstration.

discoveryNote: The source identifies January but not an exact operation day; occurredOn is a month sorting anchor.

eventTitle: RRM3 stored cryogen without boiloff before a venting issue

evidence.locator: Robotic Refueling Phase 3 and Cryogen Storage and Replenishment Demonstration sections
evidence.factSummary: RRM3 stored liquid methane for four months without boiloff; an April 8 issue then required venting, while non-fluid tool operations remained planned.

discoveryNote: One occurrence contains positive storage evidence and a transfer-program setback; mapping may create separate reviewed claims.

eventTitle: NASA began orderly shutdown of OSAM-1

evidence.locator: Announcement from NASA Headquarters section
evidence.factSummary: After notifying Congress and confirming no objection to cancellation, NASA began an orderly OSAM-1 shutdown on October 1, 2024.

discoveryNote: Program cancellation is negative historical evidence and cannot advance a capability state.

eventTitle: SERT I operated an ion engine in space

evidence.locator: SERT I development paragraph
evidence.factSummary: During a 50-minute suborbital flight, the SERT I Kaufman thruster became the first ion engine operated in space.

discoveryNote: This was a short suborbital technology demonstration, not a long-duration operational propulsion system.

eventTitle: SERT II began a long-duration orbital ion-thruster test

evidence.locator: Launch and first-thruster operation paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: NASA activated SERT II's first thruster on February 14 after its February 3 launch, beginning a months-long orbital test.

discoveryNote: Later grid shorts limited the original six-month objectives; mapping must preserve that qualification.

eventTitle: NERVA XE completed repeated ground tests

evidence.locator: Project Rover and NERVA Programs section
evidence.factSummary: The Atomic Energy Commission successfully tested the second-generation NERVA XE engine dozens of times during 1969.

discoveryNote: The source supports only year precision, and these were ground tests rather than flight evidence.

eventTitle: NERVA ended before a flight test

evidence.locator: Project Rover and NERVA Programs section
evidence.factSummary: The NERVA program was cancelled in 1973 before the engine completed any flight test.

discoveryNote: The source supports only year precision; cancellation cannot advance capability state.

eventTitle: Deep Space 1 began a multi-technology flight demonstration

evidence.locator: Opening mission account and technology-list paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: NASA launched Deep Space 1 as a flight testbed for 12 technologies, including solar-electric ion propulsion.

discoveryNote: Launch began the demonstration; a later mapping claim for successful ion operation must rely on evidence that states that outcome.

eventTitle: Dawn completed its first ion-propulsion system test

evidence.locator: First test firing and 27-hour operation paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Dawn's ion propulsion system began a 27-hour test on October 6 and operated across five throttle levels without reported performance faults.

discoveryNote: This is spacecraft checkout evidence, not by itself proof of the later full interplanetary mission outcome.

eventTitle: Viking 1 completed a controlled Mars landing

evidence.locator: Viking 1 descent and landing account
evidence.factSummary: The Viking 1 lander separated from its orbiter, descended, and made a soft landing in Mars' Chryse Planitia region.

discoveryNote: A successful planetary EDL precedent, but far below the 10-tonne heavy-cargo boundary.

eventTitle: Mars Pathfinder restored NASA surface operations on Mars

evidence.locator: Mission status landing and initial telemetry paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Mars Pathfinder landed successfully, returned health and EDL telemetry, and deployed its lander petals after touchdown.

discoveryNote: Successful robotic landing evidence, not evidence for delivery at a 10-tonne scale.

eventTitle: Curiosity completed a sky-crane landing on Mars

evidence.locator: Arrival and sky-crane landing sequence paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: After atmospheric and parachute deceleration, a powered descent stage lowered the 1,982-pound Curiosity rover to the Martian surface by cables.

discoveryNote: Integrated EDL technology evidence at rover scale, not at the 10-tonne heavy-cargo threshold.

eventTitle: Perseverance landed in Jezero Crater

evidence.locator: Touchdown confirmation and rover-mass paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: NASA confirmed Perseverance's safe Mars touchdown after a 203-day journey; the delivered rover mass was 1,026 kilograms.

discoveryNote: Successful modern Mars EDL, but the delivered mass remains well below 10 tonnes.

eventTitle: LDSD tested large supersonic decelerators with a parachute tear

evidence.locator: Second flight sequence and parachute preliminary-result paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: The LDSD inflatable decelerator deployed near Mach 3, while its 100-foot supersonic parachute developed a canopy tear near full inflation.

discoveryNote: Mixed test outcome for larger Mars payload technology; neither component delivered cargo to a planetary surface.

eventTitle: LOFTID completed an inflatable-aeroshell reentry demonstration

evidence.locator: Mission facts and successful test account
evidence.factSummary: LOFTID inflated in space, survived Earth atmospheric reentry, splashed down, and was recovered with early indications of a successful demonstration.

discoveryNote: Earth reentry test evidence for a precursor technology, not a non-Earth heavy-cargo landing.

eventTitle: Mars Polar Lander was lost during its arrival sequence
candidateTopics: planetary entry descent and landing setback

evidence.locator: Mission outcome, investigation finding, and quick facts
evidence.factSummary: Mars Polar Lander and its two Deep Space 2 probes were lost on arrival; an investigation identified a likely false touchdown signal that stopped the main engines.

discoveryNote: Negative EDL evidence; the probable cause is retained as qualified rather than stated as certain.

eventTitle: Apollo 5 demonstrated lunar-module ascent and descent propulsion

evidence.locator: Mission facts and propulsion-test description
evidence.factSummary: The uncrewed Apollo 5 flight exercised Lunar Module ascent and descent propulsion, staging, restart operations, and a simulated landing abort in Earth orbit.

discoveryNote: Integrated precursor test in Earth orbit; it did not launch from a non-Earth surface.

eventTitle: Apollo 11 carried crew from the lunar surface back to orbit

evidence.locator: Lunar ascent, orbit adjustment, and docking account
evidence.factSummary: Eagle's ascent stage launched Armstrong and Aldrin from the Moon, entered lunar orbit, adjusted its orbit, and docked with Columbia.

discoveryNote: Direct crewed surface-ascent and rendezvous evidence, but not a repeated or settlement-scale service.

eventTitle: Luna 16 launched a lunar sample toward Earth return

evidence.locator: Mission description
evidence.factSummary: Luna 16 lifted off from the Moon on September 21 and returned about 101 grams of lunar material to Earth three days later.

discoveryNote: Robotic sample-scale ascent and return, not settlement-scale cargo delivery or crew transport.

eventTitle: Apollo 1 crew died during a launch-pad test fire

evidence.locator: Incident and investigation-result paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: A flash fire in the Apollo command module during a launch-pad test killed three astronauts and led to major design, test, manufacturing, and quality-control changes.

discoveryNote: Fatal ground-test evidence and subsequent safety redesign; it cannot be treated as a capability advance by itself.

eventTitle: Apollo 13 crew returned after loss of service-module power and oxygen

evidence.locator: Opening incident, refuge, and splashdown account
evidence.factSummary: After an oxygen-tank explosion deprived the command module of power and oxygen, the three astronauts used the lunar module as refuge and splashed down safely on April 17.

discoveryNote: A real contingency and crew-recovery outcome, not evidence that the initiating failure was prevented.

eventTitle: Soyuz MS-10 aborted ascent and recovered its crew

evidence.locator: Launch anomaly, ballistic landing, and crew recovery statement
evidence.factSummary: A booster anomaly triggered a Soyuz MS-10 ascent abort and ballistic landing; search-and-rescue teams recovered both crewmembers in good condition.

discoveryNote: Operational abort and recovery evidence; it also records a launch-system failure.

eventTitle: Orion demonstrated its ascent launch-abort sequence

evidence.locator: Ascent Abort-2 result and abort-sequence paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: During Ascent Abort-2, Orion's abort motor separated the test crew module under high-stress ascent conditions, oriented it, and released it for Atlantic splashdown.

discoveryNote: Representative uncrewed abort test evidence, not an exercised operational crew rescue.

eventTitle: Crew Dragon completed an in-flight launch escape test

evidence.locator: Abort trigger, descent, splashdown, and recovery account
evidence.factSummary: Crew Dragon triggered an abort about 84 seconds after launch, descended under parachutes, splashed down about nine minutes after liftoff, and was recovered.

discoveryNote: Uncrewed integrated launch-escape and recovery test, not an operational emergency with crew aboard.

eventTitle: Gemini VIII achieved the first space docking before an emergency return

evidence.locator: Mission overview docking, tumble, stabilization, and landing paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Gemini VIII completed the first docking in space, then undocked during a violent tumble, stabilized using reentry thrusters, and made an early landing.

discoveryNote: One occurrence combines a docking milestone and a contingency response; no cargo handoff occurred.

eventTitle: Apollo and Soyuz completed an international docking and crew transfer

evidence.locator: Docking-module, docking, and joint-activity paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Apollo and Soyuz docked through a pressure-adapting module, remained joined about 47 hours, and completed four crew transfers and joint activities.

discoveryNote: Interoperability and crew-transfer precedent; it was a single bilateral mission rather than routine logistics service.

eventTitle: Atlantis docked with Mir and exchanged crews and equipment

evidence.locator: Approach, docking, crew exchange, and item-transfer account
evidence.factSummary: Atlantis made the first Shuttle-Mir docking, exchanged incoming and returning station crews, and transferred experiments, tools, and stored samples.

discoveryNote: Integrated crew and cargo handoff on one mission, but not evidence of cross-operator standardized inventory custody.

eventTitle: Dragon completed the first commercial cargo berthing at the ISS

evidence.locator: Grapple, berthing, and commercial-cargo account
evidence.factSummary: ISS crew grappled Dragon with Canadarm2 and berthed it to Harmony, making it the first commercially developed vehicle sent to the station for cargo delivery.

discoveryNote: Commercial delivery and station handoff evidence; the spacecraft used robotic berthing rather than autonomous docking.

eventTitle: Cygnus demonstrated cargo delivery and disposal logistics at the ISS

evidence.locator: Cygnus Demo 1 launch, berthing, unloading, and disposal paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Cygnus Demo 1 was grappled and berthed to Harmony, delivered 1,543 pounds of supplies, and later departed with 2,850 pounds of disposal cargo.

discoveryNote: A complete station cargo-delivery and disposal cycle, but one provider-specific demonstration rather than an interoperable logistics network.

eventTitle: ISS oxygen generation became operational

evidence.locator: Operational-history paragraph
evidence.factSummary: The ISS Oxygen Generation System entered operation to produce breathable oxygen and replace oxygen lost through station activities and leakage.

discoveryNote: Operational oxygen regeneration evidence, but not a complete closed air-and-water loop by itself.

eventTitle: ISS urine processing began producing recycled drinking water

evidence.locator: Urine Processor activation and cumulative operation paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: The ECLSS Urine Processor Assembly was activated in November 2008 and subsequently recycled crew urine into more than 22,500 pounds of drinkable water by 2015.

discoveryNote: The source supports month precision for activation; later cumulative output demonstrates continued use but not total loop closure.

eventTitle: ISS brought its Sabatier carbon-dioxide reduction system online

evidence.locator: NTRS abstract, water-balance system history
evidence.factSummary: The ISS Sabatier system came online in November 2010 to convert hydrogen and crew-derived carbon dioxide into water and methane within regenerative ECLSS operations.

discoveryNote: The source supports month precision; methane was still a discarded output, so this was not full oxygen-loop closure.

eventTitle: ISS ECLSS demonstrated 98 percent total water recovery

evidence.locator: Assessment result and Brine Processor operation paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: NASA assessed that adding the Brine Processor Assembly enabled the crew-loaded ISS water system to recover 98 percent of collected water.

discoveryNote: High-closure water-loop evidence on ISS; it does not establish equally high closure for oxygen, food, or solid waste.

eventTitle: ISS crew installed an additional carbon-dioxide recycling test rack

evidence.locator: Life Support Rack installation paragraph
evidence.factSummary: ISS crew installed ESA's Life Support Rack in 2018 to test a design for recycling carbon dioxide into oxygen.

discoveryNote: The source supports year precision and installation for testing, not a quantified closed-loop performance result.

eventTitle: ISS crew ate the first station-grown crop placed on the menu

evidence.locator: Veggie lettuce harvest and sampling paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: ISS crew harvested, cleaned, and sampled red romaine lettuce from Veggie, the first station-grown crop officially placed on the crew menu.

discoveryNote: Edible yield and consumption evidence at experiment scale, not a material fraction of crew nutrition.

eventTitle: ISS crew recovered a stressed flowering crop through onboard care

evidence.locator: Zinnia growth problem and crew-care account
evidence.factSummary: After the ISS zinnia crop showed poor condition, the crew used simplified guidance and onboard judgment to recover plants that later flowered.

discoveryNote: Useful autonomous cultivation evidence, but zinnias produced no edible yield and cannot advance food-production state alone.

eventTitle: ISS Advanced Plant Habitat produced a radish harvest

evidence.locator: Harvest, crop properties, and habitat-control paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: An astronaut harvested 20 edible radish plants grown in the ISS Advanced Plant Habitat after a 27-day crop cycle with automated environmental controls.

discoveryNote: Edible crop-yield evidence, but the harvest was stored for analysis rather than supplying routine crew meals.

eventTitle: ISS crew ate the first peppers grown in space

evidence.locator: First harvest and crop-selection paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: ISS crew harvested and ate seven chile peppers from Plant Habitat-04, the first pepper crop consumed after being grown in space.

discoveryNote: A fruiting-crop and consumption milestone at small experimental scale.

eventTitle: ISS completed a second harvest from a 137-day pepper experiment

evidence.locator: Experiment duration, second harvest, and consumption paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Plant Habitat-04 ended after 137 days with a second harvest from four pepper plants; part of the crop was eaten and part returned for analysis.

discoveryNote: Repeated harvest within one experiment, not repeated independent crop cycles or stable nutritional supply.

eventTitle: Apollo lunar crews began collecting personal radiation dose data

evidence.locator: Introduction, lessons learned, and mission-phase dose discussion
evidence.factSummary: Beginning with Apollo 8, lunar crews wore personal dosimeters that recorded mission and phase radiation doses across nine lunar flights.

discoveryNote: The source supports month precision for the first flight; no large solar-particle event occurred, so these data do not validate storm protection.

eventTitle: NASA evaluated two low-mass solar-particle storm shelter concepts

evidence.locator: NTRS abstract and publication metadata
evidence.factSummary: NASA designed centralized and crew-quarter storm shelters using habitat logistics and water walls, then performed radiation, mass, and human-in-loop evaluations.

discoveryNote: Ground design and human-factors evidence for solar-particle protection; galactic cosmic-ray protection and flight operation were not demonstrated.

eventTitle: An ISS crewmember tested a water-filled radiation shielding garment

evidence.locator: Personal shielding garment test paragraph
evidence.factSummary: An ESA astronaut tested a personal garment whose water-filled containers covered radiation-sensitive organs aboard the ISS.

discoveryNote: Wearability test in low Earth orbit; the record does not state a measured reduction in deep-space crew dose.

eventTitle: ISS missions introduced continuous personal radiation dosimetry

evidence.locator: Crew Active Dosimeters section
evidence.factSummary: NASA began using Crew Active Dosimeters on ISS missions in 2020 to continuously log, display, and downlink time-resolved individual radiation exposure.

discoveryNote: The source supports year precision; operational monitoring enables response but is not shielding or active dose mitigation.

eventTitle: Artemis I measured Orion shielding performance around the Moon
actor: NASA, DLR, and European Space Agency

evidence.locator: Detector suite, MARE experiment, shielding, and orientation findings
evidence.factSummary: Artemis I sensors and instrumented torsos measured radiation inside Orion; analysis found effective lunar-mission shielding and large orientation-dependent exposure differences.

discoveryNote: The source supports the November mission month; uncrewed lunar-duration validation does not establish long-duration or spacewalk protection.

eventTitle: Skylab 4 sustained daily exercise during a record-duration mission

evidence.locator: Endurance-record, exercise, and biomedical investigation paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: After exceeding the prior 59-day flight record, the Skylab 4 crew continued 1.5 hours of daily bicycle and treadmill exercise plus biomedical testing.

discoveryNote: Operational regimen and monitoring evidence at 84-day mission scale; the source does not quantify maintained health outcomes from exercise alone.

eventTitle: ISS resistance exercise study found improved bone and body-composition outcomes

evidence.locator: Study comparison and outcome paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Astronauts using ARED with adequate diet returned with more lean mass and maintained whole-body and regional bone mineral density compared with preflight values.

discoveryNote: Flight-population health outcome evidence, although bone breakdown still increased and diet contributed alongside exercise.

eventTitle: A two-person ISS study completed 340 days of continuous microgravity exposure

evidence.locator: Mission duration, return, and research-scope paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Scott Kelly and Mikhail Kornienko returned after 340 days on the ISS while participating in studies of weightlessness, isolation, radiation, and long-duration stress.

discoveryNote: Long-duration exposure and research cohort of two; it does not establish settlement-duration health maintenance or partial-gravity outcomes.

eventTitle: NASA published integrated molecular results from the Twins Study

evidence.locator: Integrated-study scope and key-results paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Ten teams integrated molecular, immune, telomere, gene-expression, and cognitive observations from a 340-day astronaut and his Earth-based twin control.

discoveryNote: Deep characterization from one flight subject; findings cannot be generalized as a validated population-wide countermeasure.

eventTitle: Crew physicians recognized neuro-ocular changes during a six-month ISS mission

evidence.locator: Expedition 20 observation and SANS characterization paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: During a six-month 2009 ISS mission, two physician crewmembers observed vision problems and documented optic-disc swelling and eye-shape changes now associated with SANS.

discoveryNote: The source supports year precision; this is risk-discovery evidence, not a validated prevention or treatment.

eventTitle: NASA published a water-recovery analysis for heat-melt waste compaction

evidence.locator: NTRS abstract, publication metadata, and modeled recovery phases
evidence.factSummary: NASA modeled how a heat-melt compactor could reduce trash volume, evaporate bound water for recovery, and encapsulate dried waste in a plastic puck.

discoveryNote: Ground analysis of a developing system; it does not demonstrate flight operation, potable recovered water, or reuse of the compacted material.

eventTitle: NASA evaluated thermal conversion of mixed crew waste into gases

evidence.locator: NTRS abstract, tested feedstocks, and measured products
evidence.factSummary: NASA evaluated food, packaging, hygiene items, cloth, and human-waste simulant in a thermal reactor using pyrolysis, gasification, and incineration while measuring gas and water products.

discoveryNote: Ground reactor evidence only; the work did not deliver recovered products to an operational life-support, propulsion, or manufacturing system.

eventTitle: An integrated plastic recycler and 3D printer reached the ISS
actor: NASA, Tethers Unlimited, and Northrop Grumman

evidence.locator: Refabricator description and Cygnus arrival paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: A Cygnus cargo mission delivered the Refabricator, designed to turn waste plastic and previously printed parts into filament for printing new parts aboard the ISS.

discoveryNote: Delivery and intended demonstration evidence only; this source does not report successful recycling, filament quality, or a printed end product.

eventTitle: OSCAR ran an automated trash-to-gas test in suborbital microgravity

evidence.locator: System operation, simulant boundary, and launch-history sections
evidence.factSummary: The automated OSCAR rig flew on New Shepard NS-12 and tested thermal conversion of simulated astronaut trash into syngas during about three minutes of microgravity.

discoveryNote: Brief suborbital experiment with laboratory simulant; it did not demonstrate continuous processing, useful-product delivery, or crewed-habitat integration.

eventTitle: ISS began installing a compact universal crew waste collection system

evidence.locator: NTRS abstract, installation timeline, issues, and limited checkout
evidence.factSummary: NASA began ISS installation of the Universal Waste Management System in 2020; technical issues delayed completion, and workarounds enabled only a controlled extended crew checkout.

discoveryNote: The source supports year precision. This is collection and sanitation evidence, not fecal-resource recovery or a closed solid-waste loop.

eventTitle: NASA reported the first ISS use of an onboard ultrasound proficiency aid

evidence.locator: NTRS results, Expedition 8 milestone, and publication metadata
evidence.factSummary: NASA reported successful ISS ultrasound sessions combining limited crew training, an onboard proficiency aid, and real-time guidance from ground experts; Expedition 8 first used the onboard aid.

discoveryNote: The day anchors the conference report, not the scan. Live ground expertise remained part of the method, so this was not Earth-independent diagnosis or treatment.

eventTitle: An ISS experiment generated intravenous saline from onboard potable water

evidence.locator: NTRS abstract, on-orbit production results, and concentration limits
evidence.factSummary: IVGEN produced six 1.5-liter bags of purified water aboard the ISS and mixed two with salt as normal-saline candidates using station potable water.

discoveryNote: The source supports month precision. Both saline bags missed the permitted concentration range and were returned for testing, so no operational medical use was demonstrated.

eventTitle: Untrained operators obtained diagnostic ultrasound images using prerecorded guidance

evidence.locator: NTRS methods, results, conclusion, and publication metadata
evidence.factSummary: In a ground study, untrained operators used only prerecorded audio-video instruction; eight of ten carotid studies and nearly all eye images were judged diagnostically adequate.

discoveryNote: Ground imaging study without communication support; it did not demonstrate in-flight diagnosis, treatment selection, or procedure execution beyond scanning.

eventTitle: ISS crew completed autonomous kidney and bladder ultrasound with AMOS

evidence.locator: AMOS payload result and no-ground-guidance paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: ISS crewmembers used AMOS software without the customary ground guidance and successfully acquired ultrasound images of the urinary bladder and both kidney areas.

discoveryNote: Autonomous image-acquisition evidence only; the session did not establish autonomous interpretation, diagnosis, treatment selection, or treatment delivery.

eventTitle: A second ISS crew repeated autonomous AMOS ultrasound practice

evidence.locator: AMOS crew activity and mission-autonomy paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Two ISS crewmembers used the AMOS demonstration to practice bladder and kidney ultrasound procedures as a repeat exercise in medical work without ground support.

discoveryNote: Repeated in-flight procedure-guidance evidence, but still limited to image acquisition rather than autonomous diagnosis or treatment.

eventTitle: Apollo 11 used a crewed cabin on the lunar surface for less than one day

evidence.locator: Lunar landing, surface stay, rest, and ascent paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Armstrong and Aldrin used the Lunar Module Eagle during a 21-hour, 36-minute stay on the Moon that included one EVA and a rest period before ascent.

discoveryNote: The only crew shelter was a prebuilt temporary lander cabin; no surface construction, expansion, or long-duration habitation was demonstrated.

eventTitle: Skylab reached orbit with severe thermal-shield and solar-array damage

evidence.locator: Launch damage, overheating, power shortage, and crew-delay paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Skylab lost its micrometeoroid and thermal shield during ascent, one main solar array was torn away, another jammed, and rising cabin temperatures delayed the first crew.

discoveryNote: A combined habitat thermal and power failure at launch; later crew repair prevented loss of the orbital workshop.

eventTitle: Skylab's first crew completed a 28-day orbital habitation mission

evidence.locator: Launch, docking, activity, and 28-day duration account
evidence.factSummary: Three astronauts docked with Skylab and completed a 28-day mission that included research, daily habitation, and three spacewalks.

discoveryNote: Sustained crewed orbital-habitat evidence, not construction or operation of a non-Earth surface settlement.

eventTitle: Launch of Zarya began assembly of the International Space Station

evidence.locator: Zarya launch and first-module assembly paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: The Zarya control module launched as the first ISS element, beginning a modular orbital assembly campaign that continued for 13 years.

discoveryNote: Large-scale modular assembly in Earth orbit; it does not demonstrate construction, sealing, or commissioning on a planetary surface.

eventTitle: Expedition 1 began continuous human occupancy of the ISS

evidence.locator: Expedition 1 docking and permanent-occupancy paragraph
evidence.factSummary: The three-person Expedition 1 crew docked with the ISS and began the unbroken sequence of resident international crews maintaining and operating the station.

discoveryNote: Long-running integrated orbital habitation with frequent material resupply and ground support; it is not an off-Earth surface or no-resupply demonstration.

eventTitle: NASA field-tested an integrated habitat demonstration unit in the Arizona desert

evidence.locator: NTRS abstract, prototype build, integration, and field-test account
evidence.factSummary: NASA designed, built, assembled, integrated, transported, and field-tested the functional HDU Pressurized Excursion Module during the 2010 Desert RATS campaign.

discoveryNote: The source supports year precision. This was a terrestrial analog and integration exercise, not a sealed or occupied off-Earth surface habitat.

eventTitle: A deep-space habitat analog supported delayed-communications field scenarios

evidence.locator: NTRS scenario, habitat, vehicle, bandwidth, and latency paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Desert RATS used a Deep Space Habitat, up to two exploration vehicles, three or four crewmembers, limited bandwidth, and a 100-second round-trip delay in one-day scenarios.

discoveryNote: The source supports year precision. One-day terrestrial scenarios do not establish durable surface habitation or long-duration infrastructure integration.

eventTitle: BEAM expanded to full size while attached to the ISS

evidence.locator: Mission timeline, expansion, hatch opening, and purpose sections
evidence.factSummary: The attached BEAM module expanded to full size on the ISS and was opened to crew in June for monitoring of an expandable habitat structure.

discoveryNote: An orbital structure demonstration entered only for periodic checks; it was not a continuously occupied or surface-constructed habitat.

eventTitle: NASA's habitat challenge culminated in autonomous printing of subscale structures

evidence.locator: Phase 3 completion, construction levels, and final scale paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: The final challenge phase required autonomous printing of habitat elements and culminated in one-third-scale printed structures during a terrestrial competition.

discoveryNote: The source supports year precision. Subscale ground structures were not pressure-sealed, commissioned, or built from actual off-Earth material.

eventTitle: A full-scale simulated Mars habitat was built with large-scale 3D printing

evidence.locator: Mars Dune Alpha construction and configuration paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: ICON used a large-scale printer to build NASA's 1,700-square-foot Mars Dune Alpha analog with crew quarters, workstations, and shared living areas.

discoveryNote: The source supports year precision. It is a full-scale Earth analog made under terrestrial conditions, not a pressure-qualified structure built from local lunar or Martian material.

eventTitle: Apollo 12 deployed a radioisotope power source for lunar instruments

evidence.locator: First EVA, ALSEP, and SNAP-27 deployment paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Apollo 12 astronauts deployed a SNAP-27 radioisotope generator with the ALSEP instrument package, which then operated on the lunar surface.

discoveryNote: Operational off-Earth generation for an isolated science package, not a habitat-scale grid with storage, distribution, protection, or load management.

eventTitle: ISS connected its first major U.S. solar arrays to the station power system

evidence.locator: P6 installation, array deployment, and electrical connection paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: After the P6 truss and its two arrays were installed, spacewalkers completed electrical connections that supplied solar power to the ISS U.S. segment.

discoveryNote: Generation and distribution integration on an orbital station; it is not a surface microgrid or evidence of black-start and destination-night resilience.

eventTitle: A habitat analog operated an integrated multi-bus power system for two weeks

evidence.locator: NTRS abstract, power buses, sources, controls, and campaign result
evidence.factSummary: The Desert RATS habitat power system supplied three voltage buses from a generator or two solar systems while controlling power and reporting load use during a two-week campaign.

discoveryNote: The source supports year precision. This was a terrestrial analog without off-Earth environmental cycles, settlement-scale storage, or demonstrated black start.

eventTitle: KRUSTY completed a full-power fission-system ground demonstration
actor: NASA and U.S. Department of Energy

evidence.locator: NTRS abstract, completion date, operating modes, and fault tests
evidence.factSummary: The full-scale KRUSTY ground test exercised startup, steady operation, transients, load following, passive control, and off-nominal conditions in a space-simulated environment.

discoveryNote: Integrated reactor and conversion ground evidence, but no flight, surface distribution network, storage, or settlement loads were demonstrated.

eventTitle: The first ISS roll-out solar array was connected to an operating power channel

evidence.locator: Deployment, bolting, cable connection, and channel-upgrade paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Spacewalkers deployed, secured, and electrically connected the first iROSA to the ISS P6 power channel as part of a multi-channel generation upgrade.

discoveryNote: Upgrade of a mature orbital grid; it does not validate a planetary surface grid through long darkness, dust, storage cycling, or black start.

eventTitle: EPOXI tested disruption-tolerant networking across a deep-space link

evidence.locator: DINET test, store-and-forward behavior, and image-transfer paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: The DINET experiment sent roughly 300 images between JPL network nodes and the EPOXI spacecraft using networking designed to retain and forward data across interrupted links.

discoveryNote: The source supports month precision. This was a bounded spacecraft experiment, not an operational settlement, surface, relay, and Earth network.

eventTitle: NASA documented disruption-tolerant networking implementation for the ISS

evidence.locator: NTRS architecture, implementation, integrated-test, and publication metadata
evidence.factSummary: NASA reported an ISS DTN architecture, operational-support design, implementation work, and integrated ground-test results for links subject to delay and disruption.

discoveryNote: An orbital network implementation report; it does not establish a redundant planetary surface and interplanetary communications service.

eventTitle: NASA launched its first two-way optical communications relay payload

evidence.locator: System purpose, launch, activation, and milestone sections
evidence.factSummary: LCRD launched to geosynchronous orbit and was activated as NASA's first two-way optical relay, designed for end-to-end high-rate communications experiments.

discoveryNote: Launch and initial activation of a relay payload; no planetary surface endpoint or deep-space settlement service was involved.

eventTitle: DSOC exchanged optical signals from far beyond the Moon

evidence.locator: First-light date, downlink detection, and ground receiver description
evidence.factSummary: The DSOC flight transceiver and ground optical stations achieved first light, detecting a laser downlink and exchanging test signals from beyond lunar distance.

discoveryNote: A deep-space point-to-point technology link, not an operational routed network with surface assets, redundancy, or outage handling.

eventTitle: LCRD and ILLUMA-T formed an end-to-end optical relay through the ISS

evidence.locator: ILLUMA-T first-light and end-to-end relay milestone
evidence.factSummary: ILLUMA-T on the ISS completed its first laser link with LCRD, forming NASA's first two-way end-to-end optical communications relay system.

discoveryNote: An experimental low-Earth-orbit relay chain; it did not include a planetary settlement, interplanetary delay, or long-term autonomous network management.

eventTitle: Apollo 17 exposed crew and cabin hardware to persistent lunar dust

evidence.locator: Apollo 17 visor, suit, cabin-intrusion, and health-risk paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Apollo 17 crew reported lunar dust adhering to suits and visors, scratching visibility surfaces, entering the Lunar Module atmosphere, and becoming available for inhalation.

discoveryNote: The source supports month precision. This is uncontrolled contamination evidence from a three-day sortie, not a sustained dust-prevention or removal capability.

eventTitle: Electrodynamic shields removed simulated dust in vacuum and reduced gravity tests
actor: NASA and University of Arkansas at Little Rock

evidence.locator: High-vacuum, reduced-gravity, and dust-removal test paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: NASA reported that electrodynamic dust-shield electrodes removed high levels of simulated dust from clear surfaces, radiators, panels, and fabrics in vacuum and reduced-gravity tests.

discoveryNote: The day anchors the report. Laboratory and aircraft tests did not validate lifetime, crew interfaces, or operation with actual lunar regolith on the Moon.

eventTitle: ISS activated a permanent external heat-rejection loop during power rewiring

evidence.locator: Second spacewalk, power rewiring, and thermal-loop activation paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: During STS-116 power-channel rewiring, one ISS external thermal-control loop began transporting and rejecting excess heat while converter units regulated voltages.

discoveryNote: Integrated orbital habitat heat rejection; it does not demonstrate surface thermal balance through destination day-night, seasonal, or dust conditions.

eventTitle: An ISS ammonia pump failure removed half of external cooling redundancy

evidence.locator: NTRS failure date, external thermal system, and investigation paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: An ammonia pump in the ISS external active thermal-control system failed prematurely, forcing the station to rely on the remaining cooling loop while the cause was investigated.

discoveryNote: Operational habitat thermal setback and loss of fault tolerance; continued crew safety depended on the redundant loop, power-downs, and rapid repair planning.

eventTitle: ISS restored its failed cooling loop after three contingency spacewalks

evidence.locator: Cooling Pump Shutdown section, recovery date, and three-EVA account
evidence.factSummary: After 17 days in a zero-fault-tolerant cooling configuration, three crew spacewalks installed a spare pump and restored ISS Cooling Loop A to operation.

discoveryNote: Successful on-orbit recovery required a prepositioned spare, extensive Earth support, and more than 25 hours of EVA; it was not autonomous settlement maintenance.

eventTitle: A six-person Mars analog completed eight months of isolated habitation

evidence.locator: Duration, crew, resource limits, habitat, and mission-purpose paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Six volunteers completed eight months in a small Mauna Loa habitat using shelf-stable food, limited water and electricity, and suited weekly field excursions.

discoveryNote: A terrestrial social and operations analog with normal Earth environment and external support, not integrated off-Earth infrastructure.

eventTitle: A four-person HERA crew completed a 45-day closed-habitat simulation

evidence.locator: Campaign 6 mission dates, crew, facility, and objective paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Four participants lived inside NASA's HERA analog for 45 days with bunks, work, exercise, kitchen, hygiene, and airlock areas while simulating a Phobos mission.

discoveryNote: Short terrestrial confinement research; it does not test off-Earth construction, thermal cycles, dust control, or settlement utilities.

eventTitle: CHAPEA completed a 378-day simulated Mars habitat mission

evidence.locator: Mission duration, habitat, stressors, maintenance, and egress paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: Four volunteers completed 378 days in the 1,700-square-foot Mars Dune Alpha analog while practicing maintenance, food production, limited-bandwidth work, and delayed communications.

discoveryNote: Long terrestrial analog evidence, but not an off-Earth pressure, thermal, dust, grid, or no-resupply demonstration and shorter than 26 months.

eventTitle: An electrodynamic shield removed actual regolith on the lunar surface

evidence.locator: Blue Ghost operations and EDS result paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: The Blue Ghost EDS payload used electrodynamic forces to lift and remove lunar regolith from glass and thermal-radiator test surfaces on the Moon.

discoveryNote: Uncrewed component-surface demonstration; it did not protect an occupied habitat, suit-airlock interface, mechanism fleet, or settlement-scale infrastructure.

eventTitle: Blue Ghost measured lunar dust adherence across candidate materials

evidence.locator: Blue Ghost operations and RAC result paragraphs
evidence.factSummary: The RAC instrument exposed multiple materials on the Moon and examined how lunar regolith adhered to them to inform protection of spacecraft, suits, and habitats.

discoveryNote: Field characterization and monitoring precursor only; RAC did not prevent, contain, or remove contamination.
